paragraphs: Afourer, also marketed as Nadorcott, is a late mandarin discovered in Morocco (a chance seedling thought to derive from a Murcott tree) and imported to California in 1985. It is classed as a tangor of uncertain parentage.

The flattened fruit has a thin, smooth, easy-to-peel rind and rich, sweet, juicy flesh with a refreshing acidity. It is low-seeded when grown in isolation but becomes seedy if cross-pollinated, and it holds well on the tree late into the season.

claims.text: Discovered as a chance seedling in Morocco, thought to derive from a Murcott tree, and imported to California in 1985
Also marketed as Nadorcott
Classed as a tangor of uncertain parentage
Flattened fruit with a thin, smooth, easy-to-peel rind
Rich, sweet, juicy flesh with refreshing acidity, a late-season mandarin
Low-seeded in isolation but seedy when cross-pollinated, and holds well on the tree late in the season

sources.name: Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection (UC Riverside): W. Murcott Afourer
Specialty Produce: Afourer Mandarins
Good Fruit Guide: Nadorcott (Afourer)

paragraphs: Amigo is an Australian mandarin, a probable natural hybrid of Imperial mandarin and Ellendale tangor. It arose as a seedling growing beneath an Ellendale tree on the property of Bill Foreman. It is an early to mid season variety that overlaps the Imperial season, maturing about one to two weeks after Imperial and extending into late August in the Sunraysia and Riverland districts.

The fruit is larger and more uniform in size than Imperial, with soft, juicy flesh, low to moderate rag and segments that separate easily. The skin is easy to remove and lacks the off aromas that can affect some Ellendale hybrids. Flavour is sweet, and seed counts vary from zero to more than ten per fruit, with most fruit tending to the low end.

claims.text: Australian variety, a probable natural hybrid of Imperial mandarin and Ellendale tangor that arose as a seedling beneath an Ellendale tree on Bill Foreman's property
Early to mid season, maturing about one to two weeks after Imperial and extending into late August in Sunraysia and Riverland
Larger, more uniform fruit than Imperial; easy to peel, sweet, low to moderate rag, seed number varying from zero to more than ten

sources.name: Citrus Australia: Amigo mandarin variety fact sheet
Victorian Citrus Farms: Mandarins

paragraphs: Avana is the Italian name for the common Mediterranean or Willowleaf mandarin group (Citrus deliciosa), the traditional small mandarins long grown in Sicily. The best known selection, Tardivo di Ciaculli, arose as a spontaneous late-ripening mutation near Palermo and matures up to two months later than other Avana mandarins.

The fruit is somewhat flat with a pale orange to yellow rind that peels easily. The flesh is sweet and low in seeds, with reduced central columns (bores). Maturity is very late, from January to March in the northern hemisphere, which sets the Ciaculli selection apart from the rest of the group.

claims.text: Avana is the Italian name for the common Mediterranean or Willowleaf mandarin group
The Willowleaf/common mandarin group is classified as Citrus deliciosa
The Tardivo di Ciaculli selection matures up to two months later than other Avana selections
Fruit is somewhat flat with a pale orange or yellow rind, sweet, low seeded, with reduced central columns
Maturity is January to March

sources.name: UC Riverside Citrus Variety Collection: Avana tardivo di Ciaculli mandarin
Wikipedia: Mandarin orange varieties

paragraphs: Clausellina (also written Clauselliana) is an early satsuma mandarin from Spain, a bud mutation of Owari satsuma found in 1962 at Almazora in Castellon. Its defining trait is early maturity, ripening roughly two to three weeks ahead of Owari, which made it commercially important in Spain as an early season satsuma.

Like other satsumas it is seedless and easy to peel, with sweet flesh carried on a compact tree. At full maturity it is slightly less sweet than Owari but very similar in other respects, and like many early satsumas the rind can puff if the fruit hangs too long.

claims.text: Early satsuma mandarin, a bud mutation of Owari satsuma found in 1962 at Almazora, Castellon, Spain
Matures roughly two to three weeks earlier than Owari, making it commercially important in Spain as an early satsuma
Seedless, with sweet flesh on a compact tree, slightly less sweet than Owari at full maturity

sources.name: UC Riverside Citrus Variety Collection: Clausellina Satsuma mandarin
Good Fruit Guide: Clausellina

paragraphs: The Clementine (Citrus clementina) is an easy-peel mandarin hybrid of a willowleaf mandarin and a sweet orange that appeared in late 19th century Algeria, at Misserghin, and was named after the French missionary Clement Rodier. It is one of the earliest mandarins of the season and a mainstay of winter citrus markets.

Its thin rind slips off cleanly and the flesh is sweet and juicy. The tree is self-incompatible, so fruit is largely seedless when grown in isolation, but it loses that seedlessness and turns seedy when cross-pollinated by other citrus nearby.

claims.text: Citrus clementina, a hybrid of willowleaf mandarin and sweet orange that appeared in late 19th century Algeria at Misserghin, named after Clement Rodier
One of the earliest mandarins of the season
Thin, easy-to-peel rind with sweet, juicy flesh
Self-incompatible, so largely seedless in isolation but seedy when cross-pollinated by nearby citrus

sources.name: Wikipedia: Clementine
Arab America: The Surprising Algerian History of the Clementine
Good Fruit Guide: Clemenules (Nules)

paragraphs: Marisol is an early clementine from Spain, a bud mutation of the Oroval clementine found at Bechi in Castellon in 1970. It is valued for ripening early, about two weeks ahead of Oroval, reaching colour break around late September to early October in Spain. Because it colours before it is fully ripe, it is often harvested soon after colour break and de greened.

The fruit is juicy and tender with bright colour, though it can be a little acidic when picked early. The pale skin is relatively easy to peel but tends to break. Seediness is variable. A notable quirk is that the fruit puffs and granulates if left hanging too long, so timely picking matters.

claims.text: Early clementine, a bud mutation of Oroval clementine found at Bechi, Castellon, Spain in 1970
Ripens about two weeks earlier than Oroval, around late September to early October in Spain; often harvested at colour break and de greened
Juicy and tender but can be a little acidic; pale skin peels relatively easily but breaks; variable seediness; puffs and granulates if hung too long

sources.name: UC Riverside Citrus Variety Collection: Marisol clementine
Good Fruit Guide: Marisol

paragraphs: Daisy is a US mandarin hybrid from a cross of Fortune and Fremont mandarins, named by Dowlin Young of Young's Nursery, Thermal, California, after his wife. It blends Fremont's sweetness with Fortune's acidity to give a balanced, early to mid-season fruit.

The fruit is medium to large with an attractive dark orange rind and deep-coloured flesh. It is moderately seedy, carrying roughly one to three seeds per segment, and the tree is strongly alternate bearing, which a later improved selection (DaisySL) was bred to address.

claims.text: US mandarin hybrid from a cross of Fortune and Fremont mandarins, named by Dowlin Young of Young's Nursery, Thermal, California
Early to mid-season, balancing Fremont sweetness with Fortune acidity
Medium to large fruit with an attractive dark orange rind
Moderately seedy (about one to three seeds per segment) and strongly alternate bearing

sources.name: Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection (UC Riverside): Daisy mandarin
Specialty Produce: Daisy Tangerines

paragraphs: Ellendale is an Australian tangor (a natural mandarin and sweet orange hybrid) found as a chance seedling on the Ellendale property near the Burrum River, Queensland, around 1878. It became a prized commercial variety in Australia, valued for its large size and quality.

It is a late mid-season fruit with attractive dark orange flesh that is especially juicy, sweet and rich. Seediness is variable: trees grown in solid blocks set few seeds, while those near other citrus become very seedy, and the monoembryonic seeds make it useful in breeding.

claims.text: Australian tangor (natural mandarin and sweet orange hybrid) found as a chance seedling on the Ellendale property near the Burrum River, Queensland, around 1878
Late mid-season maturity
Dark orange flesh, especially juicy, sweet and rich
Seediness variable: low-seeded in solid-block plantings, very seedy in mixed plantings; seeds are monoembryonic

sources.name: Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection (UC Riverside): Ellendale tangor
Citrus Australia: Ellendale fact sheet

paragraphs: Emperor is an old Australian mandarin thought to have originated as a seedling from fruit imported from the Orient, and it was once a leading commercial mandarin in Australia. It has served as a parent or source for other varieties, including the South African Empress.

It is a mid-season mandarin with large, oblate fruit and a puffy, loosely adherent rind that is extremely easy to peel. The light-orange flesh is tender, juicy and pleasantly sweet, though the fruit is moderately seedy and loses quality if left on the tree past maturity.

claims.text: Old Australian mandarin thought to have originated as a seedling from fruit imported from the Orient
Was once a leading commercial mandarin in Australia
Mid-season maturity
Large oblate fruit with a puffy, loosely adherent rind that is extremely easy to peel
Tender, juicy, pleasantly sweet light-orange flesh
Moderately seedy and loses quality if left on the tree past maturity

sources.name: Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection (UC Riverside): Scarlet Emperor mandarin
Citrus Australia: Emperor mandarin variety fact sheet
Daleys Fruit: Mandarin Emperor

paragraphs: Encore is a mandarin hybrid of King and Willowleaf (Mediterranean) mandarin, bred by H. B. Frost at the UC Citrus Research Center in Riverside and introduced by Cameron and Soost in 1965. It is one of the latest-ripening mandarins, often in season after most other mandarins have finished.

The medium, strongly oblate fruit has a thin, yellowish-orange rind and deep orange flesh that is firm yet tender, juicy and rich in flavour. It holds well on the tree late into the season, but is quite seedy, which limited its commercial use despite excellent eating quality.

claims.text: Hybrid of King and Willowleaf (Mediterranean) mandarin, bred by H. B. Frost at the UC Citrus Research Center, Riverside, and introduced by Cameron and Soost in 1965
One of the latest-ripening mandarins, in season after most others have finished
Medium, strongly oblate fruit with a thin yellowish-orange rind and deep orange flesh
Firm yet tender, juicy, rich-flavoured flesh
Holds well on the tree late in the season
Quite seedy, which limited its commercial use

sources.name: Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection (UC Riverside): Encore mandarin
Specialty Produce: Encore Mandarins

paragraphs: Fallglo is a US mandarin hybrid from a 1962 cross of Bower and Temple made by P. C. Reese, released by C. J. Hearn in 1987. Its parentage works out to about five eighths tangerine, one quarter orange and one eighth grapefruit, so it grows and markets as a tangerine. It is one of the earliest mandarins, maturing from late September into November, with best eating around late November.

The fruit is fairly large, usually about 2.75 to 3.25 inches across, with a deep reddish orange rind that peels with moderate ease. The flesh is sweet and juicy. The main drawback is heavy seediness, typically 20 to 40 seeds per fruit.

claims.text: US hybrid from a 1962 Bower x Temple cross by P. C. Reese, released by C. J. Hearn in 1987; genetic makeup about five eighths tangerine, one quarter orange, one eighth grapefruit
Matures late September into November, an early mandarin, with best palatability near late November
Fruit about 2.75 to 3.25 inches across with deep reddish orange rind, peels with moderate ease, and typically holds 20 to 40 seeds
Sweet, juicy flesh

sources.name: UF/IFAS EDIS: Fallglo Tangerine (HS173/CH075)
Oranges Online: Buyers Guide to Fallglo Tangerines

paragraphs: Fremont is a Clementine and Ponkan mandarin hybrid bred by P. C. Reece of the USDA at Orlando, Florida, then selected and released by J. R. Furr at Brawley, California, in 1964. It ripens early, between Clementine and Dancy, and holds its quality well past maturity.

The fruit has a bright reddish-orange rind of moderate adherence that peels reasonably well, and deep orange, tender, juicy flesh with a rich, lively flavour. It is moderately seedy, about half of the seeds being monoembryonic.

claims.text: Clementine and Ponkan mandarin hybrid bred by P. C. Reece of the USDA at Orlando, Florida, selected and released by J. R. Furr at Brawley, California, in 1964
Early-ripening, between Clementine and Dancy, holding quality well past maturity
Bright reddish-orange rind of moderate adherence, deep orange tender flesh
Rich, lively flavour; moderately seedy, about half the seeds monoembryonic

sources.name: Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection (UC Riverside): Fremont mandarin
Four Winds Growers: Fremont Mandarin

paragraphs: Hickson is an Australian mandarin that arose near Roma, Queensland, as a sporting limb on an Ellendale tangor, and it became a popular Australian commercial mandarin. It is similar to its Ellendale parent but matures mid-season, about two weeks earlier (from late June in Australia), and is reckoned to hang on the tree better.

The fruit has a smooth, glossy, yellowish-orange rind that peels more easily than Ellendale, and juicy, well-flavoured orange flesh. It is moderately seedy, typically carrying around twelve to fifteen seeds.

claims.text: Australian mandarin that arose near Roma, Queensland, as a sporting limb on an Ellendale tangor
Mid-season, maturing from late June in Australia, about two weeks earlier than Ellendale, and hangs on the tree well
Smooth, glossy, yellowish-orange rind that peels more easily than Ellendale
Juicy, well-flavoured orange flesh, moderately seedy

sources.name: Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection (UC Riverside): Hickson mandarin
Citrus Australia: Hickson fact sheet

paragraphs: Honey Murcott is a selection of the Murcott, a tangor (mandarin crossed with sweet orange) that traces to a USDA hybrid given to growers in Florida and named after Charles Murcott Smith, who propagated it in the 1920s. It is also known as the Honey tangerine.

It is a late-season mandarin prized for its very sweet, rich, honey-like flavour and high juice content, often considered one of the sweetest mandarins. The trade-off is that it is seeded and not as easy to peel as earlier varieties such as Imperial.

claims.text: A selection of the Murcott, a tangor (mandarin crossed with sweet orange) traced to a USDA hybrid propagated in Florida and named after Charles Murcott Smith in the 1920s
Also known as the Honey tangerine
Late-season mandarin
Very sweet, rich, honey-like flavour with high juice content, among the sweetest mandarins
Seeded and not as easy to peel as earlier varieties

sources.name: Wikipedia: Murcott (fruit)
Good Fruit Guide: Murcott (Honey Tangerine)
Westridge Fruit & Vegetables: Australian Mandarin Varieties

paragraphs: Imperial is an Australian mandarin that arose around 1890 at Emu Plains, west of Sydney, believed to be a chance hybrid (possibly of a Willowleaf-type mandarin with another mandarin such as Emperor). It became one of Australia's most popular commercial mandarins.

It is very early maturing, typically the first mandarin of the Australian season (around April onwards), with a thin, smooth, glossy skin that peels easily and segments that separate cleanly. The flesh is sweet and pleasantly subacid with few seeds (usually four or fewer). The tree tends toward alternate (biennial) bearing.

claims.text: Australian mandarin that arose around 1890 at Emu Plains, NSW, as a chance hybrid possibly involving a Willowleaf-type mandarin and another mandarin such as Emperor
One of the most popular commercial mandarins in Australia
Very early maturing, typically the first mandarin of the Australian season (around April onwards)
Thin, smooth, glossy skin that peels easily with cleanly separable segments
Sweet, pleasantly subacid flesh with few seeds (usually four or fewer)
Tree tends toward alternate (biennial) bearing

sources.name: Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection (UC Riverside): Imperial mandarin
Specialty Produce: Imperial Mandarins
Hill Street Grocer: All you need to know about Imperial Mandarins
Westridge Fruit & Vegetables: Australian Mandarin Varieties

paragraphs: Lemonade is a sweet, low-acid citrus that arose as a chance seedling in New Zealand in the 1980s and was quickly taken up in Australia. Its exact parentage is uncertain and often described as a lemon-mandarin type, so it is grouped here with mandarins but is not a true mandarin.

Although it looks like a rounded lemon, the yellow rind peels by hand and the flesh is sweet with only mild acidity, so the fruit is eaten fresh out of hand like a mandarin or orange rather than juiced like a lemon. It carries few to no seeds.

claims.text: Arose as a chance seedling in New Zealand in the 1980s and was quickly taken up in Australia
Exact parentage is uncertain, often described as a lemon-mandarin type
Sweet with only mild acidity, eaten fresh out of hand rather than juiced like a lemon
Yellow rind that peels by hand
Carries few to no seeds

sources.name: Wikipedia: Lemonade fruit
Specialty Produce: Lemonade Fruit

paragraphs: Miho Wase is an early satsuma mandarin (Citrus unshiu) raised in Japan as a sister nucellar seedling to Okitsu Wase and registered in 1963. Wase means early, and the fruit matures early in the season, ahead of standard Miyagawa satsumas.

Compared with Miyagawa, the somewhat oblate fruit runs higher in sugar and usually lower in acid, giving sweet, juicy flesh. Like other satsumas it is easy to peel and typically seedless. It is also valued by home growers for the cold tolerance characteristic of satsumas.

claims.text: Early satsuma mandarin (Citrus unshiu) raised in Japan as a sister nucellar seedling to Okitsu Wase, registered in 1963
Wase means early; the fruit matures early, ahead of Miyagawa satsumas
Somewhat oblate fruit, higher in sugar and usually lower in acid than Miyagawa
Easy to peel and typically seedless, like other satsumas
Valued for the cold tolerance characteristic of satsumas

sources.name: Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection (UC Riverside): Miho Wase Satsuma mandarin
Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection (UC Riverside): Okitsu Wase Satsuma mandarin
Restoring Eden: Miho Wase Satsuma Mandarin

paragraphs: Nules Clementine, also called Clemenules, is the leading Spanish Clementine selection, a mutation of the Fina Clementine found at Nules, in Castellon, around 1953. Larger and slightly earlier than Fina, it became the most widely grown Clementine in Spain and a benchmark easy-peel mandarin across Europe.

The fruit has a smooth, deeply coloured rind that lifts off easily and sweet, soft, juicy segments. It is essentially seedless when grown away from other citrus, ripens early in the season, and tends to puff if left on the tree too long after maturity.

claims.text: Also called Clemenules; a mutation of the Fina Clementine found at Nules, Castellon, Spain, around 1953
Larger and slightly earlier than Fina, and the most widely grown Clementine in Spain
Early-season, with a smooth, easy-to-peel rind and sweet, juicy segments
Essentially seedless when grown away from other citrus; tends to puff if held too long on the tree

sources.name: Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection (UC Riverside): Nules clementine
Good Fruit Guide: Clemenules (Nules)

paragraphs: Okitsu Wase is an early satsuma mandarin (Citrus unshiu) that arose as a nucellar seedling of Miyagawa, raised by M. Kajiura and T. Iwasaki at the Horticultural Research Station at Okitsu, Japan, in 1940 and registered in 1963. Wase means early, and it ripens about a week ahead of Miyagawa.

The fruit is somewhat more oblate than Miyagawa but higher in sugar, with sweet, juicy, aromatic flesh. The thin skin peels very easily and the fruit carries few seeds. It became one of the most widely planted satsuma cultivars in Japan.

claims.text: Early satsuma mandarin (Citrus unshiu), a nucellar seedling of Miyagawa raised by M. Kajiura and T. Iwasaki at the Horticultural Research Station, Okitsu, Japan, in 1940 and registered in 1963
Wase means early; ripens about a week ahead of Miyagawa
Somewhat more oblate than Miyagawa but higher in sugar, with sweet, juicy, aromatic flesh
Thin skin peels very easily and the fruit carries few seeds
Became one of the most widely planted satsuma cultivars in Japan

Comparison of four early-maturing Satsuma mandarins (NZ Journal of Crop and Horticultural Science)

paragraphs: Ortanique is a tangor (a natural mandarin crossed with sweet orange) found in Jamaica in the early 1900s, where it became an important export fruit. Its name combines orange, tangerine and unique.

It is usually late in maturity and holds very well on the tree. The medium, obovate fruit has a pebbled dark orange rind, and the orange flesh is tender, exceptionally juicy and rich and sweet. Like many tangors it is seedy when cross-pollinated, and the firmly attached rind makes it harder to peel than a true mandarin.

claims.text: A tangor (mandarin crossed with sweet orange) found in Jamaica in the early 1900s
Name combines orange, tangerine and unique
Usually late in maturity and holds very well on the tree
Medium obovate fruit with a pebbled dark orange rind
Tender, exceptionally juicy, rich and sweet orange flesh
Seedy when cross-pollinated

sources.name: Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection (UC Riverside): Ortanique tangor
Wikipedia: Tangor (Ortanique)

paragraphs: Page is a USDA hybrid from a cross of Minneola tangelo and Clementine mandarin made by Gardner and Bellows in 1942 and released in Florida in the 1960s. Because its parentage is roughly three-quarters mandarin, it is widely sold and eaten as a mandarin even though it is technically a tangelo.

The fruit is small to medium and deep orange, with a thin rind that peels easily. The flesh is notably juicy and sweet with high sugar, and it ripens early in the season. Seediness varies from few to a moderate number of seeds depending on growing conditions.

claims.text: USDA hybrid from a cross of Minneola tangelo and Clementine mandarin made by Gardner and Bellows in 1942, released in Florida in the 1960s
Parentage is roughly three-quarters mandarin, so it is treated as a mandarin despite being technically a tangelo
Small to medium deep-orange fruit with a thin rind that peels easily
Juicy, sweet, high-sugar flesh
Early-season maturity
Seediness varies from few to moderate depending on conditions

sources.name: Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection (UC Riverside): Page tangelo
Specialty Produce: Page Tangerines

paragraphs: Pixie is a seedless mandarin from UC Riverside, raised by H. B. Frost from an open pollination of Kincy (a King and Dancy cross) and released in 1965. It is prized as a very late variety that holds exceptionally well on the tree, sometimes into summer in mild districts.

The fruit has a yellow-orange, slightly pebbled rind that peels easily, and mild, sweet, juicy flesh. It is virtually seedless, which along with its late season has made it a popular home-garden and specialty mandarin.

claims.text: Seedless mandarin from UC Riverside, raised by H. B. Frost from an open pollination of Kincy (a King and Dancy cross) and released in 1965
Very late season, holding exceptionally well on the tree, sometimes into summer in mild climates
Yellow-orange, slightly pebbled rind that peels easily
Mild, sweet, juicy flesh and virtually seedless

sources.name: Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection (UC Riverside): Pixie mandarin
Wikipedia: Pixie mandarin

paragraphs: Shiranui is a Japanese mandarin hybrid of Kiyomi tangor and Ponkan, developed in Japan in 1972. It is most famous under its market name Dekopon, and is sold as Sumo Citrus in the United States and as Hallabong in South Korea. The fruit is easily recognised by a large protruding bump or knob at the stem end, the feature that gives Dekopon its name (deko meaning convex, plus pon from Ponkan).

The fruit is large and very sweet, with quality standards typically requiring a sugar level above 13 Brix and citric acid below 1.0 percent. After picking it is usually rested for 20 to 40 days so acidity falls and sugar rises before sale. It is widely sold as seedless when trees are grown away from pollen sources, although some plantings remain seedy. The rind peels readily and the season runs through winter, roughly December to February.

claims.text: Hybrid of Kiyomi tangor and Ponkan, developed in Japan in 1972
Sold as Dekopon in Japan, Sumo Citrus in the USA and Hallabong in South Korea; distinctive protruding bump at the stem end
Large and very sweet, quality standard above 13 Brix with citric acid below 1.0 percent; rested 20 to 40 days post harvest to lower acidity
Seedless when grown away from pollen sources, easy peeling, ripening December to February

sources.name: UC Riverside Citrus Variety Collection: Shiranui mandarin
Wikipedia: Dekopon

paragraphs: Silverhill is an early Satsuma mandarin (Citrus reticulata), a nucellar seedling selection of Owari raised by W. T. Swingle of the USDA around 1908 and introduced about 1931. It was field tested at Silverhill, Alabama, where it proved notably cold resistant, surviving freezes that damaged standard Satsumas.

The fruit is medium sized and slightly oblate with a thin, smooth rind, and is seedless or nearly so. The juicy flesh is high in sugars and low in acid, giving a very sweet, mild flavour. It ripens early, around November, and the tree is more upright when young than the more drooping Owari.

claims.text: Silverhill is a nucellar seedling selection of Owari raised by W. T. Swingle of the USDA around 1908 and introduced about 1931
Field tested at Silverhill, Alabama, and notably cold resistant, surviving freezes that damaged standard Satsumas
Fruit medium sized, slightly oblate, thin smooth rind, seedless or nearly so
Juice abundant, high in sugars and low in acid, very sweet
Ripens early, around November
Owari Satsuma is a well known cultivar ripening in late autumn

sources.name: UC Riverside Citrus Variety Collection: Silverhill Satsuma mandarin
Wikipedia: Mandarin orange varieties

paragraphs: Sunburst is a Florida mandarin hybrid from a cross of Robinson and Osceola, made in 1961 and released for commercial use in 1979 by C. J. Hearn of the USDA in Orlando. It became one of the most widely grown early Florida tangerines.

It is prized for a beautiful dark orange rind that is thin, smooth and fairly easy to remove. The catch is seeds: the tree is self-incompatible, so it sets poorly in solid blocks and produces seedy fruit when cross-pollinated by other citrus, needing polleniser trees nearby to crop well.

claims.text: Florida hybrid of Robinson and Osceola, crossed in 1961 and released in 1979 by C. J. Hearn of the USDA in Orlando
One of the most widely grown early Florida tangerines
Dark orange rind that is thin, smooth and fairly easy to remove
Self-incompatible, so it sets poorly in solid blocks and produces seedy fruit when cross-pollinated, requiring polleniser trees nearby

sources.name: Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection (UC Riverside): Sunburst mandarin
UF/IFAS Extension: Sunburst Tangerine (HS168/CH079)
